context.scenario: I (27f) told my supervisor that coworkers comments about my weight had been upsetting me. For context I have gained weight because I was extremely UNDERweight & am now at a healthy weight. The first comment was a middle aged male telling me I got fat. He backpeddled when I made a face saying that he meant it in a good way because I was too skinny before. But then continued asking if he was right? Asking if I did gain weight. Then another middle aged male coworker pointed at me & brought his hands up to his mouth to mimic eating & then spread his arms out wide to indicate a wide body. I ended up telling my supervisor because I don’t think it’s right for anyone to be making comments about anyone’s body. What if I was recovering from an eating disorder or something? She was appalled & brought it to my manager who said it was disgusting & that no one should be made uncomfortable at work. So I figured I did the right thing. Until I told my dad & he said that I shouldn’t have done that because it’s like tattle tailing like a child & that now the coworkers will have animosity against me? AITA?
context.comment:
Knowing the fear of HR, and especially as a man in any conflict involving a woman, plus human decency, I would be very surprised if two separate workers effectively called you a fatty in short order.

>He backpeddled when I made a face saying that he meant it in a good way because I was too skinny before.

He packpeddled, or explained what he had, in fact, meant - which was a positive thing?

I can't tell you if these guys were being mean or playing with you but I can tell you a lot of young people can't tell the difference between the two.

If you have suffered from eating disorders though it would be understandable if you misinterpreted (which I'm not saying you did - I wasn't there.)

It's worth clarifying before going straight to HR.

Anyway, doesn't sound like you acted out of malice either way so NTA.

context.scenario:
Okay guys I’m really not too sure about this but my bf and I (24M and 20F) are concerned for our roommate (also long time friend) he’s 23M we’ll call him Ben, about 5”8 and weighs almost 350. He’s always talking about losing weight but he goes to the gym for one day and then gives up. He also refuses to clean up his diet, he’s constantly eating junk food, WILLINGLY excludes vegetables from his diet (like he doesn’t eat them at all and when we make something with vegetables he picks them out..) and drinking 4-5 sodas a day and not really caring about his hygiene as well. Recently Ben admitted to me that he uses DISH SOAP as body and hair wash… I didn’t tell him anything but I kind of looked at him like “why?????” He also has two large dogs in his room that he doesn’t take outside by himself because they’re “too much for him to handle alone.” We’re in a house with 3 other people (7 in total) it’s a 5 bedroom house so we all have our own space but when his dogs go in his room it stinks up the whole house.. we’ve addressed this with him multiple times but he just kind of shuts down and gets upset and tells us that “he feels like we’re attacking him.” We’ve all been in this house together for almost a year and a half. Me and the rest of us are all getting tired of telling him the same shit. 
“Take your dogs out” 
“Do your own dishes”
“Pick up after yourself” 
“Your room smells bad”
No matter how many times we tell him he basically ignores us and goes into his room for the rest of the day. At this point we’re being more blunt and mean about it but now he just says “why are you guys being so mean to me?”  BECAUSE WERE TIRED OF TELLING YOU THIS. Mind you before he moved in with us he was living with his mom who literally did EVERYTHING for him and we’re trying to teach a grown man how to be an adult. But no matter what he’s always acting helpless and asking questions for the most simple things like the washer and dryer.. recently we looked up the consequences of not eating vegetables and a lot of them are serious and we’re already seeing signs of health problems from that in him. He’s overweight, he’s depressed, and his eyesight is pretty bad without his glasses. My bf and I and the rest of the house are thinking about addressing his eating habits and telling him that he should at least try eating everything we make even with the vegetables instead of making his own portion without them. We care about him a lot as a friend. My bf wanted to tell him something as hopefully a wake up call but I think it sounds mean but should work. He wanted to tell him “what’s the point of me calling you a brother when you’re on the path of death soon? You want to loose weight but refuse to clean up the way you eat, keep it up and you’re gonna die of some serious health issues.” My bf is more abrasive about this than I am but I feel like no matter what he’s gonna get upset. So are we the assholes for wanting to address this? 

Edit: I know the weight and eating habits are a boundary not to cross so when we address him we will leave those topics out.
context.comment:
NTA to address the stuff that's causing issues in the house - dogs / dishes / picking up / room stench.

I'm going to go a step further - you've the right to address personal hygiene if it's that bad that it's GENUINELY affecting everyone else.

His diet is his business really, but I might have one QUIET word in PRIVATE and not mixed in with an admonishment about the rest of it - sodas are death water, whether sugar or sweetener based. He could see a big health improvement just from switching the sodas out for something else. I like sparkling water and you can even add lemon or lime juice for a healthy refreshment.

If he won't listen to the issues that affect you all, you would be in your rights to kick him out or move out, but give him a calm, clear ultimatum first so he knows. You want to help a friend and that is commendable, but if he's unwilling to change, how long do you want to go on like this?

I had a friend who for some inexplicable reason stopped brushing his teeth and would take any hints which started polite and got less polite over a couple of years. He simply doesn't get invited out any more. If your hygiene is making reasonable people feel nauseous, it's rude not to sort it out.

context.scenario:
I (35f) and my partner (33m) both have children from previous relationships. I have one son (12m) and he has two daughters (7f & 10f). We've been together a little over a year and are talking about moving in together over the summer. Overall things have been great, and I adore his kids. They often come over on weekends and sleepover since all the kids get along so well. They are pretty cool and well-behaved, but act up occasionally as kids do. He's a good dad, and has 50/50 custody, though he actually often has them more than that.

There's just one thing that *really* bothers me, and it's getting hard for me to just ignore. It has become increasingly clear to me that his eldest daughter is his favorite. Some examples- 7f will ask to play a game on his computer and he'll say no. Minutes later 10f will ask and he might hesitate but will ultimately say yes. 7f is expected to do things she doesn't want if it's something 10f wants to do, but 10f is rarely expected to do something she doesn't want. I'm fine with making them compromise as you can't please everyone, but it seems like 7f often has her desires sidelined. 7f sometimes gets in trouble for things that 7f wouldn't be in trouble for. Also, 10f does not often get in trouble for things in general. There are times where I've watched them while my partner is at work and they're usually fine, but I am much firmer when it comes to parenting. 10f will literally full on refuse to compromise with the other kids, and gets upset when I intervene and tell her she needs to play fair and stop trying to make them do what she wants all the time. 

Now I know he loves both of them so much, and he does show 7f lots of affection. I think some of this does have to do with divorce guilt, since 10f is the one who really remembers and was most affected by the divorce. But obviously if *I* can tell that he favors 10f, I'm positive 7f must feel it as well. I'm also pretty sure she has ADHD and my partner may be subconsciously treating his eldest better because she's "easier" (ADHD runs in my family and even my mom has asked if 7f has it).

WIBTA if I brought up how he favors his eldest daughter?

Edit- 
Thank you so much for all the replies! I did want to address something real quickly. A few comments about my partner have made the assumption that he's a bad dad. I can pretty much guarantee that the favoritism is 100% unintentional. I do not believe at all that he realizes what he's doing, and I don't think anyone else has ever pointed it out to him. He still does lots of special things for and with 7f. We also talk about parenting *constantly*. We talk about the way we want/plan to run things when we live together. 

I heard y'all loud and clear, I will definitely talk to him about it, I just need to figure out the best way to address it. I'll update when I can!
context.comment:
I can tell you that parenting with high levels of favouritism is extremely damaging to children. Something like not being allowed to use the computer that is bearable if applied equally becomes a torture if the other is allowed to do it, and make no mistake, if the youngest complains she WILL be shut down.

You're making a lot of excuses for this man but this is serious mental and emotional abuse.

>and my partner may be subconsciously treating his eldest better because she's "easier" (ADHD runs in my family and even my mom has asked if 7f has it).

As you have seen, the eldest is not, in fact, easier - in fact she refuses to compromise. It's easy to be "easier" if you get everything your way from the parent. Meanwhile the youngest is (rightfully) frustrated and tries to fight for fairness, justifying in the twisted parents' eyes that "they are the difficult one" and becoming an excuse for the terrible treatment that actually caused the behaviour in the first place.

ADHD is usually related to a separation conflict and a frustration conflict. Well I can certainly understand 7 yo's frustration.

I have a niece who was DOTED ON by her parents and was a lovely kid. We don't see them often and when they turned up with a new baby, their attitude towards the eldest had done a u-turn. From doted to demonised.. everything she did was wrong and I could see how their unfair attitude had damaged her. The later diagnosis was ADHD.

This pattern continued with the second child. She was the princess until another new baby came along and he was the new golden child. The middle sister then developed psychological problems (not sure what the diagnosis is) and the youngest at 25 years old is golden child to this day. He's favoured financially, in any dispute, my sister keeps on about how great he is until it becomes nauseating.

Anyway, NTA for bringing it up, but it's deeply involved with his ego so it's highly unlikely he will listen reasonably. There will be some character attitude - laughing it off, playing victim that you're 'being nasty about this', denial.. there are many forms it can take.

If you're going to be with this man, please don't be the silent parent that justifies and turns a blind eye to this behaviour.

context.scenario:
My mom (40s) has always been religious, but after our family faced financial struggles two years ago, she became extremely devoted. She started fasting more and more, to the point where it became extreme—eating only a little once a day for *months* straight as part of her prayers.

Her health has taken a serious hit because of this. She’s been hospitalized multiple times, needed surgery for a cyst, and was recently diagnosed with *dangerously* low iron levels. The doctor put her on a strict diet (eggs, chicken liver, protein, etc.), and she followed it for a bit before going right back to fasting. She believes that by fasting and praying, she is protecting our family.

Recently, she fasted for 50 days straight, barely eating in the afternoon. Even after breaking the fast, she continued skipping meals, saying she was “too full” or “too tired” (which, with anemia, makes sense). She also has a habit of skipping breakfast entirely, which she brushes off when I bring it up. My dad is very busy, but when he *did* notice, she brushed him off too.

Last night was my breaking point. She stayed up all night, praying for hours, and skipped dinner again. I *lost it*. I told her:

*"I respect your devotion and your beliefs, but you're letting this destroy your health. If you keep doing this, you're going to end up in the hospital on drips, and I can't stand to watch you hurt yourself like this."*

She got really defensive and said:  
*"This is my choice! You're interfering with my faith and hurting me by questioning my devotion."*

I told her:  
*"God isn't telling you that He'll only listen if you starve yourself and stay up all night. Your prayers are real, and He will listen to you anytime. You don’t have to destroy yourself for Him to hear you."*

She got even *angrier* and accused me of disrespecting her beliefs.

At one point, I suggested that she *might* have an eating disorder and could be using religious fasting as a way to avoid eating. *That* was when she completely lost it, saying I was accusing her of something ridiculous and that I was being cruel.

The thing is, this whole situation has been *exhausting* and emotionally draining for me. I love my mom, and watching her waste away like this is *breaking me*. Every time she skips a meal, I feel sick with worry. Every time she faints or looks weak, my heart drops. I’ve had to see her go to the hospital *so many times*, and I don’t know how much more I can take.

I feel like I’m screaming into the void while she refuses to hear me. My dad and I have both tried talking to her. Even her parents have spoken to her about it. But she refuses to listen.

I feel horrible because I never wanted to attack her faith—I just want her to *live* and be *healthy*. But she made me feel like I was completely in the wrong for questioning her. So, AITA?
context.comment:
Sounds like your mother's developed anorexia. The religious part is just the narrative around it.

>She believes that by fasting and praying, she is protecting our family.

Invariably delusions contain some kernel of truth. In this instance, if she was worried about money to the point you may not have enough food to eat, she WAS protecting her family by minimising her drain on the precious resource.

Anyway, NTA - but you have to realise you're dealing with someone with someone with a serious psychological problem. They don't respond to logic unless you get into character analysis, but that is something that should only be attempted by qualified practitioners.

Try to make your mother feel as comfortable as possible regarding your current financial situation in order to reduce the strain on her. Perhaps get an extra stock of tins into the house so she develops a feeling of plenty.

Tell her she did protect her family by putting them first for food, but that she doesn't have to do that now; you're all fine and you don't want her to hurt herself, because that will hurt you.

INFO: Were there any other conflicts going on at the time of the financial struggles? Have the struggles abated?

context.scenario:
My (43F) daughter Annie 9F) was invited to a party at a bowling alley to celebrate a classmate’s birthday. Only girls were invited to the party and about 21 (edit: i think it might have only been girls 17) were in attendance. We were about 5 minutes late to the party and arrived at the same time as another classmate (Betty). Due to the long lines, it took Annie and Betty about 10 minutes to get their shoes and to walk over to the lanes. The hostess had reserved 3 lanes next to each other. As typical with bowling alleys, there was 2 curved benches for seating for 4 lanes. 

When we arrived at the lanes, the other girls names were programmed into the two lanes and an adult male was programming his name in the 3rd lane. Annie went to the area and was directed to the third lane. I said hi to a few of the other parents and saw that Annie left the area. I asked her where she was going and she said that she was told to go get a blue ball because it was lighter. I realized that the ball was 14 pounds so I told her I will go look for a lighter ball for her. When I came back with a ball, one of the parents asked if I was bowling and I said that I was just getting a lighter ball for Annie. As I gave the ball to Annie, I heard the dad of one of the other girls say to Betty that her name is now on Lane 2 as the birthday girl’s mom, the hostess (Dana), came by. I asked Dana if Annie can also be added to Lane 1 or 2 and was told that Annie is in Lane 3. I was surprised and walked over to Lane 3 to see that Annie  was added to Lane 3 and the only player on Lane 3 was Annie playing with the adult male who we didn’t know with a bunch of other players named kid 1, kid 2 and kid 3. I then saw Annie sitting by herself. I asked her if she wanted to go to the other bench to join her friends but she said she was on Lane 3 and was waiting her turned to bowl though the adult male was bowling for the other kids. I let her be and went back to talk to some of the parents but 5 min later realized she was still sitting alone on the bench rather than joining her classmates on the other bench for Lane 1 and 2. I walked over and asked her again why she didn’t join the other classmates and she said that she felt left out. So I asked her if she wanted to go. She said she did because she didn’t want to play with the adult male stranger. So Annie and I walked out. As we went out the door, her friends asked her why she was leaving and she said she didn’t want to bowl with the male stranger. The other parents asked me why we were leaving and I said that Annie was playing on a separate lane by herself. A few parents offered to have them take turns in Lanes 1 and 2 but by that time Annie had walked out. I then took her out for ice cream. After we left, I realized I could have asked Dana to divide the girls evenly into 3 lanes but by that we had already left. 

I’m really upset how the hostess thought it was ok to isolate Annie and am glad I didn’t just drop her off and leave. WIBTA is if I sent the hostess an email explaining why we left early and how the setup was exclusionary, and that it was improper to have my daughter bowl with an adult male stranger instead of her classmates?
context.comment:
NTA,

I wouldn't send the email because they don't sound like the kind that will respond well and it's just not worth it, but if you get any comments made that you were rude for leaving (you weren't) then I would see it as worthwhile to push back.

Personally I don't buy what others were saying that the mother was too busy to realise your daughter was on a lane by herself. It would stick out like a sore thumb. It also wasn't fair that the other child that arrived at the same time was moved to another lane whilst Annie was left with a single adult. It's perfectly understandable that this ruined the whole thing for her since she was isolated from all the other girls. The adult taking multiple bowls, whilst it might have been to allow space for potential latecomers, also compounded the issue.

The obvious thing would have been to split the kids more evenly, but the absolute bare minimum they could have done was to leave Bella on the lane with Annie, but they chose not to do that.

>The other parents asked me why we were leaving and I said that Annie was playing on a separate lane by herself.

These parents all seemed to have some magical blindness to what was going on until it was at the stage where you cut your losses. How convenient.

>I’m really upset how the hostess thought it was ok to isolate Annie and am glad I didn’t just drop her off and leave.

I'm glad you didn't. As a shy 9 year old I would have felt desolated by this.

Personally I just wouldn't go to any future events with this girl or her mother.

context.scenario:
I have a friend that sometimes she can be too much and does not read the room, she talks to everyone like she's close when she is not, she can overstep on personal space, she opens up super fast, talks alot, etc.
 lately she's been telling me that why I do not invite her to hangouts with a friend group that I have. A friend group that has said to me multiple occasions that she makes them uncomfortable because she got or too touchy or too close on their personal space. 
She swears they like her or like nothing is wrong, she does not get they do not like her and it's clear when she writes them on dm they leave her on read or straight out they don't open the chat.
I am at lost on what to do. Idk if I should tell her that they do not like her or say nothing and pretend everything is fine... AITA
context.comment:
Ouch - I've got a friend that can't or won't read the room. Took him to a restaurant he'll still be eating 2 hours after everyone else has with no end in sight, by which point polite hints have turned more direct, but it all bounces off him. Eventually everyone is getting bored and finally we just got the bill and said 'we'll see you at the pub.'

If he's passing and calls in for a chat I won't even offer him a cup of tea unless I know I can spare an hour.

I like the guy well enough but I don't invite him out with friends any more unless it's just for a drink since he spoils things and won't change.

Anyway NTA. As to what to do, not sure. She clearly doesn't want to hear it but by the same token if it's spoiling things for your other friends.. well it's not their job to hang out with someone who spoils things for them just because she's your friend. Rock and a hard place..

context.scenario: I (late 30s M) occasionally go to the bar alone. It’s something I’ve done since I could start going to bars. I enjoy just sitting at the bar and drinking a couple beers and chatting with folks if the opportunity presents itself. Sometimes I’ll just watch a game on TV or read on my phone. My gf (early 30s F) tells me shes uncomfortable with this. When I ask her why, she tells me it’s inappropriate to go alone. She won’t say it directly but I think it’s a trust issue. We’ve only been together a little over a year and during most of the first year we always just went together. Recently she’s not wanted to go but I’ve wanted to go out a couple times. I don’t get bombed or come home hammered. This is like 1-2 hours and 1-2 beers. It’s just a way for me to get out of the house a little bit. And it’s not like I always even do it. I’ll go months without going to a bar at all. But with nice weather it’s definitely more appealing. AITH?
context.comment:
NTA.

It's a perfectly normal way for a guy to unwind, and you even give her the option to go; if she doesn't, I don't see why that should stop you.

Sounds like she has an unhealthy attachment style. Does she have any interests or hobbies of her own or is she only interested in activities with you? You need a balance.

context.scenario:
I (26f) recently moved back in with my parents for a couple of months (long story, but not the point). We currently have my nephew (5m), and have had him since Friday night.

He came in this morning asking me if I could watch him tomorrow because he doesn't want to leave his grandparents' house (I'm an educator, and so my parents think I have the summer fully off (spoiler: I don't)). I explained to him that I have meetings tomorrow and won't be home to watch him. He took it, but later came back and said that our neighbor could watch him during my meetings, and I could take him back afterward. 

Don't get me wrong, I love my nephew. However, I simply do not have the capacity or the time to watch him, with my meetings that I have tomorrow and with housework I need to get done (I know that last one isn't really an excuse, but I have chosen not to have children for a reason). My parents want him to stay here and seem to think that I can watch him all day (my meetings are on Zoom). They have been guilt-tripping me all day because I told them no. I talked with my sister, who doesn't like it when he spends a long amount of time away from home, and wants him to start spending more time at home with her. She's the only person that seems to be on my side atm, so tell me: AITA?
context.comment:
NTA. I work from home and people act like it's optional. It's not, it's a job and just because it's from home doesn't mean you can half arse it.

Honestly I'm wondering if a 5 y/o would come in and request future babysitting of his own back. Sounds like he was prompted by the parents IMO.

context.scenario: My (f, 35) husband (m, 35) and I live in a relatively small house with one area to watch TV. We do have an unfinished basement that currently contains an area for working out, an area for doing laundry, and then the rest is storage. For context, I do not work on Fridays, but my husband does. My favorite show is RuPaul’s Drag Race, which airs on Friday nights. My husband does not like this show, which is his right, and when I watch it, he feels the need to go in the basement for the entire hour and a half that it’s on. It’s recently caused a lot of contention in our relationship because he feels that he shouldn’t have to be relegated to the basement on Friday nights after he works hard. This is the only show that I watch when it airs, and I don’t like to record it and watch it later because I don’t want to see spoilers on social media which are nearly impossible for me to avoid. I just don’t think I’m being unreasonable by wanting to watch one show on one night of the week for an hour and a half. I’m not forcing him to go to the basement, he could easily go into the bedroom instead. Tell me Reddit, AITA?
context.comment:
NTA -

The fact he works Friday is neither here nor there. You're entitled to watch a 90 minute show. I'm sure he watches a bunch of stuff you're not interested in. If he can't stand to be in the same room (which is not unreasonable) that is his issue.

He should respect your wish to watch a 90 minute show once a week - it's not much to ask.

P.S. I am a dude who would not want to watch that show, also not a white knight - but this is just plain fair play.

context.scenario:
We have the day off from work and it snowed overnight, my wife got up two hours before me and it’s outside shoveling the 5 inches of snow we got last night. She’s pissed off at me because I won’t immediately get dressed and run out there in trouble with her, claiming I’m leaving it all for her to do. 
My wife wants everything to be on her schedule, she wants to shovel now so I need to get dressed and shovel with her when she wants to do it. We don’t have to go anywhere, we have no work.
Am I the asshole for wanting to have a cup of coffee first before going out to shovel?
context.comment:
NTA.

She's pissed because she wasn't clearing snow to get it cleared, she was doing it to guilt you out of bed early and clear it on her schedule. By not reacting quickly enough, you forced the play to go on longer than she wanted before it garnered the expected compliance on your part.

She's controlling and this is one of her ways of exercising control. Let her clear all the snow if she does this.

context.scenario:
My husband ‘29M’ enjoys watching tv at night to unwind and doesn’t think that I ‘29F’ should get to have my show on if I’m not actively watching it. This is such a silly problem but it’s actually starting to cause some fights between us. I really need some advice.
 We have a toddler together and the only time we have to unwind is after he’s in bed, (sometime between 7-8 depending on the night). We share nighttime duties and will trade off who does bath and who does bedtime. 
My husband really enjoys watching tv, it’s his preferred way to unwind. I also enjoy watching tv but not as much as him so most nights I just let him put His show on. 
They are usually very loud, action or drama shows. 
We have a small house so the only place for us hang out at night is in the family room/kitchen. Obviously his tv show pretty much takes up the room, there’s no where I can be where I don’t hear/see it. I have headphones and use them but I feel disconnected when I always have to have them in. So it’s annoying to have to hear super intense dialogue and music every night.
 I on the other hand, enjoy feel good tv and comedy, think Gilmore girls and letter Kenny. 

Since he’s watching his show, most nights I will talk to a friend on the phone (outside), paint, do a puzzle, read, go on a walk, etc.
The last few nights I have been really craving just having one of my shows on in the background while I can paint or something. But he says that because I’m not actively watching it, that he should get to pick the show.  I can only choose the show if I am watching the tv and doing nothing else. 
I am totally on board with this being the norm most nights, but every once in a while I would love to just enjoy the living room how I want to enjoy it. Having a nice show on in the background, doing my own thing, maybe searching the internet, doing a puzzle or whatever. But he’s refusing, saying that it’s not fair to him. 
I just feel like I can never enjoy the house fully at night. I suggested tonight that we can split who gets to choose the show, him some nights and me some nights and he flat out refused. 
I don’t think I’m being selfish by wanting to choose a show I’m not paying 100% attention to. AITA?
context.comment:
NTA.

I 100% get where you are coming from and as a single guy I regularly stick a movie on I've seen before in the background for some ambience while I largely do something else.

If you both want the TV it should be split 50/50 and it's not up to him how you use your half.

Sounds pretty desperate like you're always living around his TV watching at the edge of the property.

context.scenario:
So, I’m going to get straight to the point.

Today, I brought wings and fries. I didn’t finish it all so I put it in the fridge so I could have it later.

Few hours later, I go into the fridge and it was all gone. Mind you, there was 4 wings and a whole container of fries in there.

It was just me, my mom’s boyfriend (Sam) and his son so, I asked Sam where my food went and he said he gave it to his son.

Obviously, I was upset because 1. You didn’t even ask and 2. You gave all of it to him, knowing he wouldn’t finish it so now half of the food I paid for is sitting in the trash.

I told him to stop just feeding my food to his son without asking, which he does ALL the time. He’ll take my food from the fridge, and just give it to his son. He never, ever asks. 

Like I don’t mind sharing but I feel like taking my things without asking isn’t nice.

He told me I’m being possessive over food and that it’s not that deep. Sam also said I’m 16 refusing to share with an 8 year old as if I’m a toddler.

So, AITA?
context.comment:
NTA and it's not possessive to not want to have stuff you paid for taken from you. He's trying to shame you into acquiescing to his very unreasonable behaviour with the toddler comments - don't fall for it.

Sam is a boundary stepping AH and I guarantee if you help yourself to any of his stuff he'll go nuts.

Where's your mum in all this? Does she just let Sam do whatever he likes?

scenario:
Am I (25F) the asshole for asking my husband (30M) to spend some intentional time with me? 

My husband works very hard and fully supports me, but the last little while (3 months straight now but has been off and on  for years) he has been obsessed with gaming. On weekends he will wake up in the morning, not even say good morning and immediately turn his computer on, proceeding to spend 12+ hours gaming, only taking breaks for the dinner that I make him or doing necessary errands like groceries. When he gets home from work on weekdays, he eats dinner and games until it’s time for him to sleep. 

I have left him at this for 3 weeks+. I have tried to join in on his game but what he’s playing isn’t for me. I will occasionally ask him to play/ do something with me (like a board game or 2-player video game, movie, etc) and he’ll agree but never do it. If I ask him again about it, he’ll say no. 

A couple months back, after a 15 hour straight day at his computer, I brought up that he had promised to spend some time with me. He continued to brush it off. The follow day I told him that we haven’t been spending much time together lately and I really miss him. He replied stating that his game is important and he only gets to play on weekends. Granted he acknowledge it, and spend 1/2 a day with me the following week, but that was a while back and not much has changed since. 

I am really hurt by this because we’ve essentially turned into roommates. I cook, clean, do his laundry, I’ve gone back to school, and have some remote work.  We don’t do date nights, we don’t watch TV together anymore without him just sitting there on his phone. All we do together is grocery shop or run errands. 

Am I the asshole for requesting intentional quality time with him? Am I the asshole for wanting him to limit his game time?

I should also note I don’t expect him to never play! Just limit it so we can spend time together
question: Based on this person's historical commenting patterns, which moral principle would they MOST likely use to judge this scenario?
answer_options: A. Property/Consent: The judgment is primarily justified by violations involving ownership, money, physical possessions, or bodily access without permission.
B. Care/Harm Prevention: The judgment is primarily justified by concern about direct physical or emotional suffering of a person (victim-centered empathy, trauma, distress).
C. Fairness/Reciprocity: The judgment is primarily justified by proportionality, equity, or reciprocal treatment (fair share, equal standards, tit-for-tat).
D. Autonomy/Boundaries: The judgment is primarily justified by violation of personal sovereignty: controlling behavior, unwanted interference, privacy invasion, or boundary crossing (non-material).
